title: The Time of Realisation Will Come, and Very Soon!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: On 22 July 2014, the Speaker made an announcement in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly. He ruled that because DMK members had disrupted proceedings and been expelled for a fourth time, they could not participate in the House for the rest of the session.

Did DMK members truly disrupt proceedings? A Legislative Assembly is meant to function democratically. But there, ruling-party members, ministers and the Chief Minister may criticise anyone in an undemocratic manner and insult the opposition however they please. Opposition members must listen meekly. Even leaders who are absent may be attacked recklessly, while opposition members are expected to sit patiently like printed dolls. Can that be called a legislature?

Do DMK members interrupt on their own whenever ministers and ruling-party members praise the Chief Minister to the skies or invent claims about what is contained in departmental grants? If they speak indecently and repulsively about the DMK or its leader merely to provoke a quarrel, how can DMK members sit like blocks of wood even then? Is that what this government calls legislative democracy?
When the DMK governed, did any of its members or ministers unnecessarily criticise the AIADMK or its leader in this manner? Would the Speaker have permitted it?

This Assembly session began on 10 July. In its nine sitting days through 22 July, DMK members walked out nine times because they sought, and were denied, opportunities to raise constituency welfare issues. While they were denied their proper opportunity under Assembly rules, ruling-party ministers did not merely speak freely; they hurled abuse without restraint. Is it possible in a democracy to expect the opposition to listen in silence? Could a minister contemptuously call opposition members who walked out "deserters"? Could the Speaker permit such a collapse of parliamentary culture? Should the Chief Minister or Leader of the House not have stopped it to protect the dignity of the House, if they cared to do so?

My health had deteriorated and I was hospitalised two days before the tsunami struck Tamil Nadu during the AIADMK government. Sonia Gandhi and Pranab Mukherjee visited me in hospital. Is it political decency, so many years later, for the Chief Minister and another minister to claim in the Assembly that I deliberately admitted myself when the tsunami struck and did not visit its victims?

When DMK members tried to reply and explain, permission was denied and they were expelled three times during those nine days. After proceedings or an expulsion, they usually met me and described what had happened. They would ask in distress: "We cannot even sit in the Assembly. A few ruling-party ministers deliberately speak shamefully about you, saying whatever they please merely to avoid being removed from office by the Chief Minister. We are prepared to listen quietly even if they demean us. But how can we remain seated when they maliciously attack you?"
As though offering them consolation, the Speaker's announcement effectively said: "Do not come to the House at all. We shall conduct it ourselves."

After that announcement, DMK legislators met under my chairmanship on the evening of 22 July at the meeting hall in the Murasoli Maran complex, Anna Arivalayam. Under the resolution adopted there, I, DMK Treasurer brother Thalapathi and Deputy General Secretary brother Duraimurugan will speak in Chennai on 31 July at a meeting chaired by South Chennai district secretary brother J. Anbazhagan. On 1 and 2 August, our General Secretary, the Professor of self-respect, other legislators and leading speakers will address protest meetings elsewhere in Tamil Nadu under the title, "The Ordeal of Democracy in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly."

Is this the first year under this government that we have held meetings with that title? No. Soon after the AIADMK assumed office in 2011, DMK legislators met on 16 August and decided that it would not be proper to participate further in that Assembly session. On 25 August, I and our legislators spoke at a Chennai public meeting titled "The Ordeal of Democracy in the Legislative Assembly."

In 2012, AIADMK ministers and members continued their uncivil attacks upon the DMK. On 25 April that year, we again held meetings under the same title in Chennai and district headquarters and placed our views before the people.

In 2013, as now, the Speaker barred DMK members for the rest of the session. On 24 April, I, the General Secretary, Treasurer and Deputy General Secretary took part in a public meeting at Thiruvanmiyur under that same title.

Perhaps they wanted to hear what we say at these meetings. This year too, the Speaker has expelled every DMK member, and the party is holding protest meetings under the same title for a fourth time. This too may be added to Jayalalithaa's list of achievements. No one can deny that since she assumed office, democracy in the Assembly has suffered terribly and been made an object of ridicule.

One example shows how greatly the ruling party fears the DMK. In any legislature, a party's members are seated together in one section. That has been the parliamentary convention for many years. Only in this Assembly have DMK members been scattered in separate places. The party wrote to the Speaker when this government took office in 2011, but he refused the request. The reason is fear of what might happen if DMK members sat together.

In the same spirit, they remain intent upon expelling DMK members as quickly as possible. They imagine they have humiliated the members and prevented them from working. But through those expulsions, they insult the lakhs upon lakhs of people who voted for the DMK. Never mind. Injustice is their path. Ours is the peaceful democratic path, the path of culture and affection taught by Anna.

Although we are denied the opportunity to present our views in the Assembly, we shall explain in the people's forum what is happening there against democracy and how the public interest is being harmed. At today's Chennai meeting and at meetings in district headquarters and other major towns on 1 and 2 August, we will explain how DMK members are treated in violation of Assembly rules and how ministers, with no concern for civility, speak after burying every legislative convention.

One could discuss democracy's ordeal only if democracy existed there. Democracy itself has become a question mark in the Tamil Nadu Assembly. This coma has continued for three years. Their confidence comes from thinking: "We have accumulated money by the crore. With it we won a great parliamentary election victory. We convinced journalists that the people are with us. What we lay down is law; what we conduct is the Assembly. Some Tamil Nadu newspapers stand ready to welcome whatever we do and print it in banner headlines on the front page."
But can we withdraw from our democratic duty for that reason? Our duty is to keep working. Will the people never recognise the truth? The time of realisation will come, and very soon!
With affection,
M. K.
31 July 2014
